questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The Archive on Wheels
When the county museum bought a van, some residents wondered why a museum needed a vehicle instead of another display case. The answer became clear during the first “scan day” at the Pine Street Senior Center. Museum staff set up folding tables, laptop scanners, and labeled envelopes. Residents brought photographs, church programs, restaurant menus, military letters, and school certificates that had been stored for years in drawers and boxes.
The museum did not keep the originals. Instead, staff scanned the items, recorded basic information, and returned everything to the owners. The digital copies became part of a community archive that researchers, students, and families could use. The goal was to collect stories that rarely appear in official records.
One photograph showed a grocery store that had been replaced by a bank. A handwritten menu listed meals from a café that closed before most current residents were born. A stack of letters described a factory worker’s move from Mississippi to the county in the 1950s. Each item added a small piece to the local record.
The project also revealed a challenge. Some people were unsure about sharing family materials, especially if the items included names, addresses, or painful memories. Museum staff created permission forms that let owners choose whether copies could be public, restricted to researchers, or kept private until a later date.
The van allowed the museum to reach neighborhoods instead of waiting for residents to come downtown. In doing so, it changed the archive from a place people visited into a service that visited them.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: A mobile museum project helped preserve local history by scanning residents’ materials while respecting ownership and privacy.
choices.B: The county museum bought a van to replace all of its permanent exhibits.
choices.C: Residents were required to donate original family documents to the museum.
choices.D: The museum stopped collecting local history because residents lacked photographs.

questions.correctAnswerText: A mobile museum project helped preserve local history by scanning residents’ materials while respecting ownership and privacy.
questions.explanation: The passage explains that the van project collected digital copies of local materials while returning originals and allowing privacy choices.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Archive on Wheels.”
Why did the museum scan items instead of keeping the originals?

choices.A: To create digital copies while allowing owners to keep their materials
choices.B: To avoid recording any information about the items
choices.C: To sell the original documents to researchers
choices.D: To prevent families from using their own photographs

questions.correctAnswerText: To create digital copies while allowing owners to keep their materials
questions.explanation: Staff scanned items for the archive and returned the originals to residents.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The Archive on Wheels.”
Which TWO items were brought to the scan day?

choices.A: Church programs
choices.B: Restaurant menus
choices.C: New computers from the museum office
choices.D: Construction permits for the museum van

questions.correctAnswerText: Church programs | Restaurant menus
questions.explanation: The passage lists church programs and restaurant menus among the items residents brought.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Archive on Wheels.”
What can be inferred from the museum’s use of permission forms?

choices.A: Staff understood that some historical materials may include sensitive personal information.
choices.B: Staff wanted to make all private materials public immediately.
choices.C: Residents were not allowed to decide how their materials would be used.
choices.D: The museum refused to accept any item that mentioned a family member.

questions.correctAnswerText: Staff understood that some historical materials may include sensitive personal information.
questions.explanation: Permission forms were created because some materials included names, addresses, or difficult memories.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Archive on Wheels.”
In the sentence “Each item added a small piece to the local record,” local record most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=history of the community
questions.explanation: In context, local record means the collected history of the community.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Archive on Wheels.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Residents brought photographs, menus, letters, and certificates.
tiles.B: Staff returned the original items to their owners.
tiles.C: Owners could choose public, restricted, or later access.
tiles.D: The museum required every family to give up original documents.

targets.T1: Evidence that the archive included personal materials
targets.T2: Evidence that residents kept ownership of their materials
targets.T3: Evidence that privacy choices were built into the project

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Residents brought photographs, menus, letters, and certificates.; T2=Staff returned the original items to their owners.; T3=Owners could choose public, restricted, or later access.
questions.explanation: The tiles show personal materials, owner control, and privacy choices.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Lab Sample Labeling Procedure
To: Clinic Assistants, Nurses, and Lab Couriers
From: Medical Office Supervisor
Subject: Updated Lab Sample Labeling Procedure
Beginning November 3, all lab samples must be labeled before they leave the exam room. The label must include the patient’s full name, date of birth, collection date, collection time, and the initials of the employee who collected the sample.
Before placing the label on the container, the employee must compare the printed label with the patient’s wristband or photo identification. If any information does not match, stop and notify the nurse in charge. Do not correct the label by hand unless the nurse in charge gives approval and documents the correction.
Samples must be placed in the correct transport bag. Blood samples, urine samples, and swabs may require different bags or forms. Couriers should refuse any sample that is missing required information or is not sealed properly.
This procedure is intended to prevent delays, repeat collections, and identification errors. A sample that cannot be matched clearly to a patient may be rejected by the lab. Questions about label printing or transport bags should be directed to the medical office supervisor before samples are collected.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain how staff should label, verify, and transport lab samples
choices.B: To tell couriers to accept every sample regardless of its condition
choices.C: To allow staff to write any patient name on a container after it leaves the room
choices.D: To announce that the clinic will stop collecting lab samples

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain how staff should label, verify, and transport lab samples
questions.explanation: The document gives steps for labeling, checking identification, and transporting samples properly.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lab Sample Labeling Procedure.”
What should an employee do if the printed label does not match the patient’s identification?

choices.A: Stop and notify the nurse in charge.
choices.B: Send the sample to the lab immediately.
choices.C: Remove the patient’s date of birth from the label.
choices.D: Ask the courier to guess which label is correct.

questions.correctAnswerText: Stop and notify the nurse in charge.
questions.explanation: The procedure says to stop and notify the nurse in charge if information does not match.

questions.prompt:

Refer to “Lab Sample Labeling Procedure.”
Which TWO details must appear on the label?

choices.A: The patient’s full name
choices.B: The collection time
choices.C: The courier’s home address
choices.D: The patient’s favorite pharmacy
choices.E: The name of the clinic’s insurance company

questions.correctAnswerText: The patient’s full name | The collection time
questions.explanation: The required label information includes the patient’s full name and collection time.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lab Sample Labeling Procedure.”
Why may a lab reject a sample?

choices.A: It cannot be matched clearly to a patient.
choices.B: It was collected before November 3.
choices.C: It was placed in a sealed transport bag.
choices.D: It includes the collector’s initials.

questions.correctAnswerText: It cannot be matched clearly to a patient.
questions.explanation: A sample that cannot be matched clearly to a patient may be rejected by the lab.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Lab Sample Labeling Procedure.”
Which sentence best explains the reason for the updated procedure?

choices.A: “Beginning November 3, all lab samples must be labeled before they leave the exam room.”
choices.B: “The label must include the patient’s full name, date of birth, collection date, collection time, and the initials of the employee who collected the sample.”
choices.C: “This procedure is intended to prevent delays, repeat collections, and identification errors.”
choices.D: “Questions about label printing or transport bags should be directed to the medical office supervisor.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “This procedure is intended to prevent delays, repeat collections, and identification errors.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly explains the reason for the updated procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Give Part-Time Workers Schedules Earlier
Employers should give part-time workers their schedules at least two weeks in advance whenever possible. Many part-time workers arrange childcare, transportation, second jobs, school assignments, and medical appointments around their work hours. When schedules change with little notice, a worker may lose wages, miss class, or pay extra for last-minute childcare.
Advance scheduling also benefits employers. Workers who know their hours earlier can plan transportation and arrive on time. They are less likely to trade shifts at the last minute or call out because of avoidable conflicts. A store manager may not be able to predict every rush or absence, but a basic schedule posted earlier gives everyone a clearer starting point.
Some employers worry that two-week schedules reduce flexibility. That concern is understandable in restaurants, retail stores, and service businesses where customer traffic can change quickly. A fair policy could allow true emergencies and limited adjustments while still discouraging unnecessary last-minute changes. Employers could also ask workers to update availability monthly so managers have accurate information before building schedules.
Predictable scheduling is not only a worker issue. It is a planning issue. When employees can organize the rest of their lives, they are more likely to be reliable at work. Posting schedules earlier is a reasonable step toward a workplace that respects both business needs and workers’ time.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: Employers should provide part-time schedules earlier because predictability helps workers and can improve reliability.
choices.B: Part-time workers should never be allowed to change availability.
choices.C: Employers should close restaurants and stores when schedules are difficult.
choices.D: Workers should be required to accept schedule changes without notice.

questions.correctAnswerText: Employers should provide part-time schedules earlier because predictability helps workers and can improve reliability.
questions.explanation: The author argues that advance schedules help workers plan and help employers reduce avoidable conflicts.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Give Part-Time Workers Schedules Earlier.”

choices.A: Workers may lose wages, miss class, or pay extra for last-minute childcare.
choices.B: Managers can ask workers to update availability monthly.
choices.C: Customer traffic can change quickly.
choices.D: Advance scheduling gives employers a clearer starting point.

questions.correctAnswerText: Workers may lose wages, miss class, or pay extra for last-minute childcare.
questions.explanation: This detail supports the claim that short-notice schedules can create real problems for workers.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Give Part-Time Workers Schedules Earlier.”
Which TWO employer benefits does the author describe?

choices.A: Workers may be more likely to arrive on time.
choices.B: Workers may be less likely to trade shifts at the last minute.
choices.C: Employers can stop training new workers.
choices.D: Employers can ignore emergencies.
choices.E: Managers will never need to adjust a schedule.

questions.correctAnswerText: Workers may be more likely to arrive on time. | Workers may be less likely to trade shifts at the last minute.
questions.explanation: The passage describes both as possible employer benefits of earlier scheduling.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: By suggesting a policy that allows true emergencies and limited adjustments
choices.B: By saying businesses should never respond to customer traffic
choices.C: By arguing that managers should stop making schedules
choices.D: By claiming that workers should not have outside responsibilities

questions.correctAnswerText: By suggesting a policy that allows true emergencies and limited adjustments
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges flexibility concerns and proposes a balanced policy.

questions.correct: D1=In a survey of local childcare providers, most said they charged extra when parents requested care with less than forty-eight hours’ notice.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In a survey of local childcare providers, most said they charged extra when parents requested care with less than forty-eight hours’ notice.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by showing a cost of last-minute scheduling.

questions.prompt:

Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Many part-time workers have responsibilities outside work that require planning.
choices.B: Every business can predict customer traffic perfectly.
choices.C: Workers with advance schedules will never become sick.
choices.D: Part-time jobs are always temporary and unimportant.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many part-time workers have responsibilities outside work that require planning.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that predictable schedules help workers manage responsibilities outside work.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The First Broadcast
Maya had volunteered at the community radio station for a month before anyone let her near the microphone. Until then, she labeled music files, answered phones during pledge hour, and watched the hosts speak as if their words had been waiting calmly in their pockets.
On Thursday, Mr. Bell handed her a script for the noon community announcements. “Three minutes,” he said. “Read slowly. Smile when you speak, even if nobody can see you.”
Maya sat in the small booth with headphones pressed against her ears. Through the glass, Mr. Bell pointed to the red light. Her first sentence came out too fast, and the word “neighborhood” tangled in her mouth. Heat climbed into her face. She imagined every listener turning the dial.
Then she saw the sticky note Mr. Bell had placed beside the script: “Pause. Breathe. Continue.”
Maya took one breath, then another. She read the next announcement more slowly. A food pantry had new hours. A youth theater group needed volunteers. A street near the post office would close for repairs. The words became separate pieces instead of one long rope.
After the red light went dark, Mr. Bell opened the booth door. “You corrected yourself on air,” he said. “That matters more than being perfect.”
Later that afternoon, Maya answered the station phone. A caller asked for the theater group’s number. Maya wrote it down carefully and smiled, though the caller could not see her.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Maya must manage her nervousness during her first live radio announcement.
choices.B: Maya tries to convince Mr. Bell to cancel the community announcements.
choices.C: Maya argues with a caller about the theater group’s number.
choices.D: Maya refuses to volunteer at the radio station after pledge hour.

questions.correctAnswerText: Maya must manage her nervousness during her first live radio announcement.
questions.explanation: The excerpt centers on Maya’s anxiety and recovery during her first time on air.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Broadcast.”
Why does the author compare the words to “one long rope”?

choices.A: To show that Maya initially feels the announcements running together too quickly
choices.B: To explain that the script is tied to the microphone
choices.C: To show that the station broadcasts only outdoor programs
choices.D: To suggest that Maya has forgotten how to read

questions.correctAnswerText: To show that Maya initially feels the announcements running together too quickly
questions.explanation: The phrase shows that Maya feels rushed until she slows down.

choices.A: It helps her slow down and continue after making a mistake.
choices.B: It causes her to leave the booth immediately.
choices.C: It tells her to skip the rest of the announcements.
choices.D: It makes her stop answering phones.

questions.correctAnswerText: It helps her slow down and continue after making a mistake.
questions.explanation: The note reminds Maya to pause, breathe, and continue, helping her recover.

choices.A: She reads the next announcement more slowly.
choices.B: She smiles while helping the caller later that afternoon.
choices.C: She imagines every listener turning the dial.
choices.D: She labels music files before going on air.
choices.E: She refuses to speak after the first sentence.

questions.correctAnswerText: She reads the next announcement more slowly. | She smiles while helping the caller later that afternoon.
questions.explanation: These details show Maya becoming calmer and more confident.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The First Broadcast.”
What does Mr. Bell mean when he says, “You corrected yourself on air”?

choices.A: Maya recovered from a mistake while the broadcast was still happening.
choices.B: Maya rewrote the entire script before entering the booth.
choices.C: Maya corrected the caller’s phone number.
choices.D: Maya changed the station’s broadcast schedule.

questions.correctAnswerText: Maya recovered from a mistake while the broadcast was still happening.
questions.explanation: Mr. Bell praises Maya for continuing and correcting herself during the live announcement.

questions.prompt:

Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Maya’s first sentence comes out too fast.

tiles.C: Maya gives a caller the theater group’s number.
tiles.D: Maya shuts off the station’s transmitter.

questions.explanation: The tiles show Maya’s initial difficulty, helpful guidance, and later calm performance.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Elevator Inspection Tags
Most people step into an elevator without noticing the small inspection tag posted inside or near the door. The tag is not decoration. It shows that the elevator has been inspected according to local safety rules. While inspection systems vary by city or state, most tags include the inspection date, the expiration date, the elevator identification number, and the agency or company responsible for the inspection.
The expiration date is especially important. It does not necessarily mean the elevator becomes unsafe the next day, but it does mean the inspection is no longer current. Building managers are responsible for scheduling inspections and correcting problems that inspectors identify. If an elevator has an expired tag, residents or visitors can report it to building management or the local inspection office.
An inspection tag does not guarantee that an elevator will never have a mechanical issue. Like cars and other machines, elevators can develop problems between inspections. Strange noises, uneven stops, doors that do not close properly, or repeated service interruptions should be reported even if the tag is current.
Inspection tags help the public see that a safety process exists. They also provide information that makes reporting easier. A person who can give the elevator identification number and the expiration date can help managers or inspectors locate the correct equipment quickly.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Elevator inspection tags provide safety and reporting information, but people should still report problems between inspections.
choices.B: Elevator inspection tags are decorations that do not include useful information.
choices.C: An elevator with a current tag can never develop a mechanical issue.
choices.D: Visitors are responsible for inspecting elevators themselves.

questions.correctAnswerText: Elevator inspection tags provide safety and reporting information, but people should still report problems between inspections.
questions.explanation: The passage explains what tags show and why problems should still be reported.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Elevator Inspection Tags.”

choices.A: The inspection is no longer current.
choices.B: The elevator must be replaced immediately.
choices.C: The elevator identification number has disappeared.
choices.D: Visitors may inspect the elevator themselves.

questions.correctAnswerText: The inspection is no longer current.
questions.explanation: The passage says an expired date means the inspection is no longer current.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: Doors that do not close properly
choices.B: A tag that lists an elevator identification number
choices.C: A building manager scheduling an inspection
choices.D: A visitor reading the inspection date

questions.correctAnswerText: Doors that do not close properly
questions.explanation: The passage lists doors that do not close properly as a problem to report even if the tag is current.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Elevator Inspection Tags.”
Which detail best supports the idea that tags make reporting easier?

choices.A: A person can provide the elevator identification number and expiration date.
choices.B: Most people step into elevators without noticing the tag.
choices.C: Inspection systems vary by city or state.
choices.D: Elevators can develop problems between inspections.

questions.correctAnswerText: A person can provide the elevator identification number and expiration date.
questions.explanation: This information helps managers or inspectors locate the correct equipment quickly.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Elevator Inspection Tags.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It explains what inspection tags show, why dates matter, and when problems should be reported.
choices.B: It tells a story about a person trapped in an elevator.
choices.C: It compares elevators in apartment buildings with elevators in shopping centers.
choices.D: It argues that inspection tags should be removed from public view.

questions.correctAnswerText: It explains what inspection tags show, why dates matter, and when problems should be reported.
questions.explanation: The passage defines tag information, explains expiration dates, and gives reporting guidance.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Safety Vest Pickup
(1) The River Trail Cleanup will provide safety vests for all registered volunteers. (2) Volunteers should pick up a vest at the check-in tent before beginning work. (3) The vests help team leaders identify cleanup crews along the trail. (4) [D1], volunteers should return the vests to the same tent before leaving. (5) The returned vests [D2] counted and cleaned for the next event. (6) Any vest with a broken zipper should be placed in the repair bin.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=After the cleanup; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “After the cleanup” correctly indicates when vests should be returned, and “will be counted and cleaned” is grammatically correct.

choices.A: Any vest with a broken zipper should be placed in the repair bin.
choices.B: Any vest that has a zipper that is broken should be put into the bin that is for repairs.
choices.C: Vests with broken zippers, because they are broken, should be placed in the repair bin for repair.
choices.D: The repair bin is where any vest should be placed if that vest has a broken zipper.

questions.correctAnswerText: Any vest with a broken zipper should be placed in the repair bin.
questions.explanation: This sentence is the most concise while preserving the meaning.

choices.D: The list of cleanup zones have been updated.

questions.explanation: Repeating “Rachel” makes clear who confirmed the time.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: The supply truck arrived late; however, the cleanup started on time.
choices.B: The supply truck arrived late however the cleanup started on time.
choices.C: The supply truck arrived late, however the cleanup started on time.
choices.D: The supply truck arrived late; however the cleanup, started on time.

questions.correctAnswerText: The supply truck arrived late; however, the cleanup started on time.
questions.explanation: A semicolon before “however” and a comma after it correctly join the two independent clauses.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a complete sentence?

choices.B: After the storm cleared from the area.
choices.C: The team finished clearing branches by noon.
choices.D: While carrying gloves and trash bags.

choices.C: The captain checked the map and marked the meeting point yesterday.

choices.C: The orientation explains to sign in, sorting supplies, and reports about hazards.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Add Evening Family Swim Hours
The community pool should add evening family swim hours twice a week during the summer. Many parents and caregivers work during the day and cannot bring children to afternoon swim sessions. Evening hours would give more families access to a public facility that they already help support through taxes and fees.
Family swim also supports water safety. Children become more comfortable around water when they have supervised opportunities to practice basic skills. Not every family can afford private lessons, and some children need time in the pool before they are ready for formal instruction. Evening hours would not replace swim classes, but they would create more chances for families to build confidence together.
Pool staff could manage crowding by requiring registration, limiting the number of swimmers, and reserving one lane for adult lap swimmers. The pool already stays open late for occasional swim meets, so evening operation is possible. A carefully planned schedule would make the pool more useful to the community.
Passage B: Keep Pool Hours Focused on Lessons and Lap Swim
The community pool should not add evening family swim hours this summer. The pool already has a tight schedule that includes swim lessons, lap swim, lifeguard training, and maintenance. Adding family swim in the evening would require more lifeguards, more cleaning time, and additional supervision during the busiest part of the day.
The current schedule serves important needs. Swim lessons provide structured instruction that improves water safety more effectively than open swim. Lap swim supports adults who rely on the pool for low-impact exercise after work. Lifeguard training keeps the facility staffed and safe. Reducing any of these programs could hurt the very community the pool is meant to serve.
The pool should explore family access, but not before studying staffing and cost. A better first step would be a one-day weekend pilot program with registration. That would let the parks department measure demand without disrupting lessons or lap swim. Good intentions are not enough; the pool needs a schedule it can operate safely.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports adding evening family swim hours, while Passage B argues that the pool should protect existing programs and study demand first.
choices.B: Passage A argues that pools should close during summer, while Passage B argues that children should never swim.
choices.C: Passage A supports private lessons only, while Passage B supports eliminating lifeguards.
choices.D: Passage A focuses on playground repairs, while Passage B focuses on library programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports adding evening family swim hours, while Passage B argues that the pool should protect existing programs and study demand first.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether evening family swim should be added now.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about community pool hours.

choices.A: Many parents and caregivers work during the day and cannot bring children to afternoon swim sessions.
choices.B: Lifeguard training keeps the facility staffed and safe.
choices.C: The pool has a tight schedule.
choices.D: A one-day weekend pilot could measure demand.

questions.correctAnswerText: Many parents and caregivers work during the day and cannot bring children to afternoon swim sessions.
questions.explanation: This detail directly supports Passage A’s argument for evening access.

choices.A: Adding evening family swim would require more lifeguards, cleaning time, and supervision.
choices.B: Swim lessons provide structured instruction that supports water safety.
choices.C: Families never use public pools in the summer.
choices.D: Lap swimmers should not be allowed to use public facilities.
choices.E: Evening pool operation is impossible under all circumstances.

questions.correctAnswerText: Adding evening family swim would require more lifeguards, cleaning time, and supervision. | Swim lessons provide structured instruction that supports water safety.
questions.explanation: Passage B supports both claims with specific reasons.

Refer to the paired passages about community pool hours.

choices.A: “Many parents and caregivers work during the day.”

choices.C: “Pool staff could manage crowding by requiring registration, limiting the number of swimmers, and reserving one lane for adult lap swimmers.”
choices.D: “Evening hours would not replace swim classes.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Pool staff could manage crowding by requiring registration, limiting the number of swimmers, and reserving one lane for adult lap swimmers.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly addresses concerns about crowding and shared use.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about community pool hours.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors value water safety, but they disagree about whether evening family swim is the right way to support it.
choices.B: Both authors believe the community pool should be sold to a private company.
choices.C: Both authors argue that swim lessons are unnecessary.
choices.D: Both authors think families should avoid public pools.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors value water safety, but they disagree about whether evening family swim is the right way to support it.
questions.explanation: Passage A says family swim supports safety, while Passage B emphasizes structured lessons and safe scheduling.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about community pool hours.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on evening staffing costs, current pool attendance, and family demand for later swim times
choices.B: A list of swimsuit colors sold in nearby stores
choices.C: A history of swimming competitions in other states
choices.D: A map of hiking trails near the community center

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on evening staffing costs, current pool attendance, and family demand for later swim times
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate both the need for evening family swim and the practicality of offering it.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Stacked beside the doorway, Jordan moved the folding tables into the hall.

choices.A: Stacked beside the doorway, Jordan moved the folding tables into the hall.
choices.B: Jordan moved the folding tables stacked beside the doorway into the hall.
choices.C: Jordan, stacked beside the doorway, moved the folding tables into the hall.
choices.D: The doorway moved Jordan’s folding tables stacked into the hall.

questions.correctAnswerText: Jordan moved the folding tables stacked beside the doorway into the hall.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “folding tables,” the items stacked beside the doorway.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The center posted a map. The map showed emergency exits.

choices.A: The center posted a map that showed emergency exits.
choices.B: The map posted the center and showed emergency exits.
choices.C: Showing the center, emergency exits posted a map.
choices.D: The center posted, and emergency exits showed the map.

questions.correctAnswerText: The center posted a map that showed emergency exits.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses an apostrophe correctly?

choices.A: The nurse’s notes were placed in the patient file before the lab pickup.
choices.B: The nurses notes were placed in the patient file before the lab pickup.
choices.C: The nurses’ note’s were placed in the patient file before the lab pickup.
choices.D: The nurse notes’ were placed in the patient file before the lab pickup.

questions.correctAnswerText: The nurse’s notes were placed in the patient file before the lab pickup.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe correctly shows that the notes belong to one nurse.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Provide Childcare at Community College Campuses
Community colleges should provide on-campus childcare for students with young children. Many students who attend community college are working adults, parents, or caregivers. For these students, tuition is only one barrier. A parent may be ready to attend class but unable to enroll because childcare is unavailable, unreliable, or scheduled at the wrong hours.
On-campus childcare would make education more realistic for student parents. A parent could drop off a child near class, attend a lab or lecture, and respond quickly if a problem occurs. The childcare center could also serve students in early childhood education programs by offering supervised observation or work-study opportunities. In this way, the service could support both parents and future childcare workers.
Some colleges may worry about cost, staffing, and liability. Those concerns are serious, but they can be managed with careful planning. Colleges could begin with limited hours, prioritize students with the greatest need, charge sliding-scale fees, and seek grants or partnerships with local childcare organizations. A small program would not solve every scheduling problem, but it could help students who might otherwise drop out or never enroll.
Community colleges are designed to expand opportunity. Providing childcare directly supports that mission by removing a barrier that affects many capable students.
Passage B:
Focus College Resources on Academic Support
Community colleges should not make on-campus childcare a priority. Childcare is important, but colleges already struggle to fund tutoring, advising, technology, career services, and course availability. If limited money is spent on a childcare center, fewer resources may remain for services that benefit all students.
Running childcare is also complex. A college would need licensed staff, safe facilities, insurance, health procedures, emergency plans, and age-appropriate materials. These requirements are not optional, and they can be expensive. A poorly funded childcare center could create safety risks and damage trust. Colleges should not add a service unless they can operate it consistently and well.
There are better ways to support student parents without building a childcare program. Colleges can schedule more evening, weekend, and hybrid classes. They can help students find community childcare providers, connect parents with public assistance, and create emergency grants for childcare costs during exams or clinical placements. These supports are flexible and may reach students at multiple campuses.
The goal should be to help student parents complete their education, but on-campus childcare is not the only solution. Colleges should first strengthen academic support and connect students to existing childcare resources.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether community colleges should provide on-campus childcare? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
